replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's a completely different system, and not wholly compatible with the system taught in The Druidry Handbook and The Druid Magic Handbook. It's very tempting to add on book after book, system after system -- I remember! -- but it's a better idea to focus on one system at a time, master it, then go on to something else. The Druidry Handbook and The Druid Magic Handbook are both teaching material from the same system, so you can combine them; more advanced practices in the same system will be out next year when my book The Dolmen Arch is published; but I'd encourage you to stick to those until you've got a good handle on the system, and only then assess other forms of magic.

2) It really depends on your personal schedule. Me, I do my magical training first thing in the AM, and physical exercises in the evening, but that's just what works for me. Try different schedules and see what works for you. As for trying the rituals in a kata style, give it a try and see what results you get!

3) Divination is a challenge -- learning to use it without becoming dependent on it is one of the basic tasks of the training. To borrow a phrase from another spiritual system, it can be a worthy opponent. Whether that's a challenge that's right for you right now is another matter, and one I can't answer for you.

replies.title: divination with cards
replies.content:
If you do not already have a regular divination practice, and the idea of using cards for divination worries you, there are lots of divinatory methods that don't use cards. Some of them are much older than card reading, since sets of things made out of paper only become affordable about five hundred years ago.

As you probably know already, geomancy does not require cards, and there are divinatory systems that use sticks, cowrie shells, stones, tea leaves, pendulums, and the positions of stars and planets. People make up new ones from time to time. With the exception of astrology, most systems of divination combine some random element, a limited set of symbols, and the use of mental associations or intuition to develop an answer.

(1) In my chart several planets have positive aspects, but also are afflicted, two very severely (viz., by sign). Does such a severe affliction cancel out their positive influence to a great extent, perhaps altogether, or does the upside still stand? How does one make such a determination?

replies.title: Re: Totally, utterly catastrophic horoscope of Doom
replies.content:
1) The afflictions don't cancel out the dignities, or vice versa. Let's say you have Venus in Virgo trine Saturn in Capricorn. Venus in Virgo, otherwise uninfluenced, will tend to give you whopping problems with your love life -- that placement tends to make those who have it shy away from emotional and physical intimacy, and hide their feelings behind an overly critical and perfectionistic mask. The trine with Saturn means that you'll be able to get around this via a relationship with an older partner, or one who's physically unattractive by conventional standards -- in either case, one who won't set off the same negative reactions. So treat the positive aspects as ways to work around the negative dignities.

2) You are going to die someday, by some means. A sudden accidental death means that you won't spend the last decade of your life shriveling up physically and mentally in a nursing home. Why are you complaining?

comments.title: Natural Magic
comments.content:
Hi!

I am interested in buying your book on natural magic. However I live in the southern hemisphere where the flora, climate and plant floklore can be different than in the northern hemisphere.

(1) Is that a significant problem?

(2) Have you had any feedback from people who have used your book from the southern hemisphere?

(3) If so, can you share some of them?

Thanks and regards

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content:
A lot depends on where in the southern hemisphere you live. In Australia and New Zealand, a lot of European plants were naturalized in the 18th and 19th centuries, so you'll have some common ground to go on. Elsewhere, not so much.

The broader issue, of course, is that every region has its own flora and its own natural magic. My book is specifically based on medieval and Renaissance European lore, which is also fairly applicable to the eastern half or so of North America -- the Atlantic Ocean is fairly recent in geological terms, and there's a lot of shared ecology. Other parts of the world will have to work out their own herbal magic. My book may still be useful to you in terms of the techniques, but you may need to go looking for information on traditional uses of your native plants.

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content: Perhaps this is relevant: how did Culpeper determine the astrological properties of American plants?

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content: The art of astrological pharmacognosy was well understood in his time, and taught to physicians as a matter of course. It's basically been completely forgotten at this point, and the few modern books on the doctrine of signatures (the basis of the process) are frankly very poor. A great deal still remains to be relearned!

As a ceremonial mage, I tend to default to the ritual methods -- the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram or the Sphere of Protection ritual are the two methods I've used, both with good effect. Other traditions of magical and spiritual work have their own methods. Your experience with the healer shows the purpose of that kind of work: the aura (the outer layer of your subtle body) becomes a boundary that you can control, deciding what gets in and what doesn't. In an age of confusion and decline like this one, having that in place is essential!

Wells' "War of the Worlds". So Stalin concocted a plot where the Flying Wing would be brought to the US by another plane using remote control, and somewhere in public, a bunch of aliens would step out of it. Those aliens, according to this book, were victims who had been surgically altered by the infamous Dr. Josef Mengele, at Stalin's behest, to resemble the almond-eyed "grays" that are now so famous. But the remote control system failed and the Flying Wing crashed in New Mexico instead of Dallas, or Times Square.

Intriguing to think that two of history's greatest monsters, Stalin and Mengele, might have shaped pretty much the entire American science-fiction industry today -- by planting a dramatic suggestion about an invasion from space by LGM's. replies.title: Re: Is divination mandatory?-

comments.title: Trees
comments.content: What is the ethical way to cut down trees for things like building material and firewood when one acknowledges that trees have consciousness?

replies.title: Re: Trees
replies.content:
If I may inject some thoughts here... If you have access to a large enough woodland, you will more or less always find a few dead trees. Where I live, for example, you always find a few rather thin oaks that possibly found it too dark beneath the roof of the surrounding beeches or died by some other reason. If they stood long enough, the bark and a large part of the outer few cm of trunk and branches will be eaten up by insects and fungi but the core remains intact for decades, even in a shady and wet forest, and you can harvest very valuable and already dried firewood or you can use it as a very durable building material. If you find dead spruce (bark-beetle!), pine, or similar, you have to be much (!) quicker with your harvest than with oaks or beeches, since the wood is not so durable...

If you can't avoid to cut a living tree, you can at least see whether it's possible to improve the life of the surrounding trees giving them more light, space, etc. Also it is possible to look for damaged or unhealthy trees. Still, those dead and damaged trees feed an army of insects and fungi, so by cutting them, you take their food out of the forest.

To my mind, the best way to harvest wood ethically is to use the wood responsibly. Do not use more than you need. If you build things, make them sturdy so they last as long as possible. If your construction fails after some time (especially outdoors), use the remaining parts to build other things or use it as firewood. Do not convert wood to waste by using ugly chemical paint, laze, etc. Do not heat more, than you really need. If you take a living tree consider planting a new one, for example in a clearing. Or you could protect young trees against deer, etc. Of course you can also try to communicate with the trees and the forest to find out what's best.

Myself, I still fall short to my own expectations frequently. It always bothers me, if I notice that I wasted wood. The more wood I cut and chop and burn and build with over the time, the more I think about how to live, so that it's possible to use less wood. And the more it bothers me... And more and more I realize in what a sad state our whole society really is...

Cheers!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Fate is the sum total of the influence of all your past decisions, in this and previous lives; it's an exact equivalent of the concept of karma. Destiny is the specific goal toward which your Higher Self is striving, the unique self that you will eventually be. Will is the third factor, which sometimes mediates between them, sometimes decides to favor one or the other, and sometimes gets ganged up on by both the others.

comments.title: Nail biting and magic
comments.content:
I want to use the LBRP to get rid of my onicophagia. I've come to visualize my compulsion as a crustacean, but I feel like I need to visualize it more clearly. I haven't gotten the most satisfying results yet. Also, do you know how should I do the projection of it un the ritual? On what part of it? Also, do I make the sign of silence immediately after the projection sign, or after the whole ritual?
Alternatively, something along the lines of writing something on paper and doing something to it that I could try?

Thanks in advance

JP

replies.title: Re: Nail biting and magic
replies.content:
The LBRP is not a ritual for changing habits. It's a ritual for general purification and cleansing of your aura. I know some people have tried to use it in recent years for things like changing habits, but from what I've heard, their results are generally pretty much the same as yours.

To get rid of a compulsive habit you have to understand the emotional drives and the childhood or past-life memories that gave you the habit -- otherwise all your attempts to drive it away will simply make the same thing come back again in a different form. You can't deal with something like this by repressing it, with or without magic!

What I'd recommend is that you start a dialogue with the habit. This is best done in writing. Get a blank notebook and a pen, write the words "Why do I bite my nails?" and then write down whatever comes to mind, no matter how absurd or uncomfortable it might be. Keep asking questions; don't argue, just encourage the "other voice" in the conversation to express its feelings. Spend some time doing this every day, *especially* when you find yourself coming up with every possible reason not to. it's by getting to know your own subconscious that something like this can be resolved.
